title: From Siloed Algorithms to Compliance‑First Agentic Platforms: A Multi‑Layered Architecture for Hospital AI Systems
venue: International Journal of Innovative Science and Research Technology (IJISRT)
summary: Hospitals are rapidly adopting artificial intelligence for triage, imaging, scheduling etc., yet most deployments remain isolated point solutions locked inside departmental silos, resulting in duplicated effort, hidden risks, and unrealised enterprise value. Despite explosive growth of AI in healthcare market and accelerating investment, an estimated 70–80% of healthcare AI pilots fail to scale, largely due to governance gaps, fragmented data, and missing integration blueprints.

title: Early Online
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly reshaping clinical practice, and has prompted the Health Professions Council of South Africa to publish Booklet 20 – its first ethical framework for AI use in healthcare. This review critically evaluates Booklet 20 through the lens of the South African Medical Association AI Task Team, examining its alignment with national legislation, emerging regulatory mechanisms, broader policy commitments and the ethico-social context.

title: Evidence-based AI: from trailblazer to trustblazer?
summary: Agentic AI systems can plan, call tools, and coordinate specialized sub-agents, enabling multi-step scientific workflows that exceed what single-model text generation can reliably deliver. Yet in high-stakes domains such as regulatory science and toxicology, fluent outputs are not sufficient: adoption hinges on traceability, reproducibility, context-of-use validity, and explicit uncertainty communication.

title: Trust, Identity, and Continuous Attestation for Autonomous Neural Agents: An Integrated Framework Mapping the EVOLENTITY Infrastructure to Machine-Learning Evaluation Metrics
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
summary: Abstract Autonomous neural agents are moving rapidly from research prototypes to operational components of digital services, where they perceive, decide, and act with limited human oversight. This shift creates a structural tension: conventional machine-learning evaluation quantifies algorithmic and statistical quality, yet it does not capture whether an agent can be identified, trusted, held accountable, or certified for operation in regulated environments.

title: AI-Enhanced CICD Governance for Regulated Cloud Applications: A Compliance-Aware DevOps Framework
venue: International Journal of Science and Research Archive
summary: AI-supported CI/CD for regulated cloud applications has become an important area of research because modern delivery pipelines now function not only as engineering infrastructure, but also as compliance-producing systems. Release automation has to produce defensible evidence, as opposed to just making deployment faster, in sectors where safety, privacy, security, and accountability are required.

title: Human–AI hybrid finance: from AI tools to decision systems
summary: Abstract Artificial intelligence (AI) in finance is commonly reviewed by method, data type, or application domain. These perspectives are essential, but they understate a deeper shift: AI is moving from a predictive tool to a component of human–AI hybrid financial decision systems.

title: Untitled work
venue: International Journal of Science Strategic Management and Technology
summary: Mental health issues are among the most neglected problems in public health and globally, and treatment gaps are significant even among the wealthy nations.The development of artificial intelligence and multi-agent systems can change the accessibility and enable privacy-preserving personalization of mental health support.This paper describes a Hybrid Agentic AI Framework which consists of a Random Forest ensemble classifier, DistilRoBERTa-based Emotion Detection Agent, a Severity Stratification Agent, and a FLAN-T5

title: A Thematic Qualitative Literature Review of Risk Controls, Compliance Frameworks, and Auditability in Enterprise GRC
venue: Journal of information technology, cybersecurity, and artificial intelligence.
summary: This structured qualitative literature review examines how generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) governance is operationalized in enterprise governance, risk, and compliance (GRC). The review analyzed 37 studies, standards, regulatory materials, scholarly preprints, and professional guidance documents, with substantive GenAI governance and control literature concentrated in 2019-2026.

title: Auditing Artificial Intelligence-Driven Financial Systems: Accountability, Transparency, and Auditor Liability in Algorithm-Based Decision Making
summary: Purpose: This study examines how the growing use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in financial reporting and auditing affects audit reliability, accountability, and transparency. It focuses on key challenges such as AI’s “black box” nature, outdated auditing standards, limited auditor expertise, and unclear legal responsibility.

title: Dissociative Identity: Language Model Agents Lack Grounding for Reputation Mechanisms
summary: As autonomous language model agents proliferate, forming an emerging agentic web with real-world consequences, what credibility signals can you use to decide whether to trust an unfamiliar agent in the wild and delegate to it? A natural governance intuition is to extend human identity verification and reputation mechanisms, from ``Know Your Customer'' and credit scores to ``Know Your Agent'' regimes.

title: Quantum–AI Systems Thinking through Vedic Wisdom: Strategic, Mathematical, Ecological, and Cognitive Health Perspectives
summary: This academic manuscript develops an interdisciplinary framework for Quantum-AI systems thinking through Vedic wisdom, with strategic, mathematical, ecological and cognitive health perspectives. The book treats Vedic wisdom as an interpretive and ethical knowledge tradition, not as a literal substitute for contemporary physics or empirical science, and positions quantum-AI as a family of uncertainty-aware, optimisation-oriented and adaptive computational methods.

title: From Recommender to Actor: The Normative Boundary When RAG Tools Become Tool-Calling Agents
summary: Abstract Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems increasingly operate not only as tools for retrieving and synthesizing information, but also as agents that can invoke external functions, modify digital environments, and execute tasks across software systems. This development raises a specific normative problem: the point at which a model’s output ceases to be merely informational and becomes an executable intervention in the world.

title: Sparse Feature Geometry of Transformer Circuits for Model Editing, Alignment and Behavioural Control
summary: Abstract Sparse feature geometry has become a decisive frontier for interpreting, editing, aligning and controlling transformer systems because it treats behaviour not as a monolithic property of a model, but as an emergent consequence of distributed, partially sparse and context-sensitive circuits. This manuscript develops a doctoral-level framework for studying transformer circuits through feature spaces, attribution structures, causal interventions, predictive trustworthiness models, reproducible engineering pip

title: AI-driven personalization of food systems: From precision nutrition to climate-resilient supply chains
summary: Traditional large-scale production and standardized interventions cannot address the risks due to individual metabolic diversity, climate change, and supply chain disruptions. This article reviews how artificial intelligence (AI) technology synergistically enhances precision nutrition interventions and climate-resilient supply chain resilience through cross-scale data integration and algorithm optimization, breaking away from the fragmented analytical approach that traditionally separates nutrition science from sup

title: GateLens: A reasoning-enhanced LLM agent for automotive software release analytics
summary: Ensuring reliable data-driven decisions is crucial in domains where analytical accuracy directly impacts safety, compliance, or operational outcomes. Decision support in such domains relies on large tabular datasets, where manual analysis is slow, costly, and error-prone.

title: A Systematic Lifecycle-Referenced Capability Mapping of MLOps Platforms for Energy Forecasting
summary: Accurate energy forecasting is essential for maintaining power system reliability, integrating renewable generation, and ensuring market stability. Although machine learning has improved forecasting accuracy, its operational deployment depends on Machine Learning Operations (MLOps) platforms that automate and scale the entire lifecycle of energy data pipelines.

title: AI-Driven Service Intelligence Models for Operational Excellence and Customer Experience Optimization in Service Economies
summary: Abstract: This manuscript develops a unified academic framework for understanding how artificial intelligence can be integrated into service systems to improve operational excellence and customer experience without collapsing explanation into automation or governance into afterthought. It treats service intelligence as a structured field of inquiry at the intersection of operations, statistics, machine learning, data engineering, and institutional design.

title: Trustworthy AI Posture (TAIP): A Framework for Continuous AI Assurance of Agentic Systems at Horizontal and Vertical scale
summary: The emergence of autonomous, high-velocity Agentic AI systems is creating an internal assurance scalability crisis. Point-in-time, document-based audits cannot keep pace with non deterministic behaviour and distributed deployments of agents across rapidly evolving environments.

title: From privacy to trust in the agentic era: a taxonomy of challenges in trustworthy federated learning through the lens of trust report 2.0
summary: • Trustworthy AI is a prerequisite for deploying FL in high-risk, multi-stakeholder domains. • Privacy-preserving guarantees alone are insufficient to sustain trust in agentic and dynamic FL systems.

title: Evaluating and Regulating Agentic AI: A Study of Benchmarks, Metrics, and Regulation
summary: Agentic AI represents a new generation of Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems capable of perceiving, reasoning, planning, and acting toward achieving goals with a degree of autonomy. Unlike traditional AI models that merely generate outputs, these systems maintain memory, interact with their environment, and adapt over time.

title: Unified Security Orchestration Framework for Protecting AI/ML Systems Against Input-Based and Model Supply Chain Attacks
summary: The quick implementation of Large Language Models (LLMs) in companies and vital systems has exposed new types of security concerns that are not solvable by current cybersecurity tools. These tools are now vulnerable to adversarial attacks like prompt injection, indirect manipulation of input, multi-turn exploits and compromises of the supply chains of models.

title: Embedding governance in AI security culture: from trust calibration to accountable decisions
summary: This study develops and validates a multidimensional framework of security culture tailored to AI-enabled decision-making in public administration and higher education. Drawing on a mixed-methods design combining qualitative elicitation, psychometric validation, structural equation modeling, and experimental testing, the research identifies five interrelated cultural dimensions: role clarity and accountability in human–AI collaboration, psychological safety to contest AI outputs, awareness of bias and corrective ro

title: Survey on LLM Safety: Attacks, Defenses, Alignment, Metrics, and Guardrails
summary: Abstract Large Language Models (LLM) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across various applications, but their deployment raises critical safety concerns as potential misuse poses significant societal risks. This survey reviews the end-to-end security and safety pipeline of LLMs, focusing on the interaction between users and model responses.

title: Governing Generative AI in Healthcare: A Normative Conceptual Framework for Epistemic Authority, Trust, and the Architecture of Responsibility
summary: BACKGROUND/OBJECTIVES: Large language models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT are rapidly being integrated into healthcare for tasks ranging from clinical documentation to diagnostic support. Current ethical discussions focus predominantly on bias, privacy, and accuracy, leaving three critical governance questions unresolved: What kind of knowledge does an LLM output represent in clinical reasoning?

title: Security and Privacy of Large Language Models: Threat Taxonomy, Ethical Implications, and Governance
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed across professional and societal domains, introducing security, privacy, and governance challenges beyond traditional software vulnerabilities. Despite extensive research on individual risk categories, a unified lifecycle-oriented perspective connecting architectural properties, adversarial threats, and governance implications remains limited.

title: The Audit Gap: Why Existing Assurance Frameworks Fail for AI Systems and What Comes Next
venue: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)
summary: The global assurance and audit industry - generating over $250 billion annually and built around frameworks like SOC 2, ISO 27001, ISO 42001, and financial auditing standards - was designed for deterministic systems with predictable, reproducible outputs. Artificial intelligence systems are fundamentally different: they are probabilistic, their behavior shifts with data distribution changes, their decision logic is often opaque even to their developers, and their performance degrades over time through mechanisms (m

title: When trust becomes symbolic: standards and the governance of AI
summary: Purpose This paper aims to reconceptualise standardisation as a generative governance arrangement that organises expectations, trust and decision-making under persistent uncertainty. Drawing on Niklas Luhmann's systems theory and extending Kim's (2025a) concept of standards as thematic identities and expectational structures, it seeks to explain how standards operate beyond technical coordination.

title: TAXAL framework: Triadic fusion of cognitive, functional, and causal dimensions for explainability in agentic LLMs
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-risk domains where opacity, bias, and instability undermine trust and accountability. Traditional explainability methods, often centered on surface outputs, are insufficient for capturing the reasoning pathways, planning logic, and broader sociotechnical implications of agentic LLMs.

title: Auditing clinical AI in oncology: Strengthening assurance frameworks and nursing leadership in the Asia–Pacific context
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly being used in cancer care across the Asia-Pacific region.However, AI tools that work well in one country or hospital can fail in another because of differences in patients, equipment, language, and clinical practice.This commentary explains why three different checks-validation (does it work for our patients?),verification (is it built correctly?), and auditing (is it safe and fair over time?)-are all needed to ensure AI is used safely.We argue that oncology nurses, who

title: Deception and Communication in Autonomous Multi-Agent Systems: An Experimental Study with Among Us
summary: As large language models are deployed as autonomous agents, their capacity for strategic deception raises core questions for coordination, reliability, and safety in multi-goal, multi-agent systems. We study deception and communication in LLM agents through the social deduction game Among Us, a cooperative-competitive environment.

title: Data curation in academic libraries: navigating the AI labyrinth through the FATE principles
venue: Information Research an international electronic journal
summary: Introduction. This study examines how academic libraries address the ethical and operational challenges of data curation in an era shaped by artificial intelligence (AI).

title: Biotechnology and Gene Editing: Genomic Engineering, Biological Systems, and Precision Therapeutic Platforms
summary: Abstract This manuscript develops a doctoral-level account of biotechnology and gene editing as a convergence field linking genomic engineering, biological systems science and precision therapeutic platforms. It frames gene editing not as a discrete laboratory technique but as a research-governance architecture that integrates molecular design, statistical inference, machine learning, omics data engineering, translational validation, manufacturing quality and responsible deployment.
